Event: Portuguese initiate organized slave raids in Africa
Description: First systematic capture and trade of Africans, setting stage for transatlantic system; ~10 million eventually transported.

Event: First enslaved Africans arrive in Americas
Description: Spanish colonies import slaves for labor in mines and plantations, expanding European involvement.

Event: First Africans arrive in English North America
Description: 20 Africans sold in Virginia, marking start of chattel slavery in what becomes the US.

Event: US Constitution drafted with slavery protections
Description: 3/5 compromise counts enslaved people for representation, entrenching system under new republic.
Frees slaves in Confederate states during Civil War, but full abolition waits until 1865; ~4 million freed.
Nazi Party seizes power, beginning anti-Jewish policies; leads to boycotts and camp openings like Dachau.
Strip Jews of citizenship and rights, legalizing discrimination; affects 500,000+ German Jews.
Nationwide violence destroys synagogues and businesses; 30,000 Jews arrested, signaling escalation.
Coordinates mass murder of 11 million European Jews; leads to death camps like Auschwitz.

Event: Allied liberation of camps; Hitler suicide
Leader/President: Franklin D. Roosevelt / Harry Truman (US), Winston Churchill (UK), Joseph Stalin (USSR)
Description: ~6 million Jews killed; camps freed, ending Holocaust but exposing systemic genocide.

Event: Federal Reserve Act creates central bank
Description: Enables modern credit system, laying groundwork for consumer debt expansion.

Event: Airline Deregulation Act boosts credit access
Description: Sparks widespread credit card use; household debt begins rapid rise from $300B to trillions.
Removes interest rate caps, fueling consumer lending boom; debt hits $1T by 1985.
Allows banks to mix commercial/investment activities, enabling risky loans and subprime traps.
Household debt peaks at $14T; foreclosures trap millions in mortgage bondage, echoing debt peonage.
Stimulus and unemployment push debt to $14.5T+; exacerbates inequalities, with 1 in 5 Americans in collection.
